title: Permutation and model-based importance
scope: Two importance families. Details the difference between built-in model importance (e.g., Gini impurity for trees) and model-agnostic permutation importance.
key_concepts: Model-based importance calculates feature contributions intrinsically during training (e.g., node purity improvements in trees).
Model-based methods are fast but can be biased toward high-cardinality features and only reflect training data.
Permutation importance is calculated post-training by randomly shuffling a feature's values and measuring the drop in a specific performance metric.
Permutation importance is model-agnostic and, when run on validation data, avoids overfitting biases.
definitions: Model-based importance: Importance scores generated organically by the algorithm during the fitting process.
Permutation importance: An evaluation method that measures a feature's utility by computing how much the model's error increases when that feature's data is randomly shuffled.
examples: A Random Forest might assign high importance to a high-cardinality ID column. Running permutation importance on a hold-out validation set reveals shuffling the ID doesn't hurt performance, proving it was overfitted.
relations_to_other_chapters: Connects to Ch 7 (Feature Selection) where these metrics are often used to filter features.
what_to_exclude: Mathematical derivations of Gini impurity or entropy.
